exercises.title: Migration risk analysis
exercises.instructions:
Rate each migration approach for Zerodha's 10-year-old trading monolith (1=safest, 5=most dangerous):
A: Strangler Fig (incremental, proxy-based, feature flags)
B: Big Bang Rewrite (complete rewrite, switch all users at once)
C: Parallel Run (both systems active, gradual traffic shift)
D: Feature Branch (develop new system in a separate code branch for 1 year)
E: In-Place Refactoring (rewrite modules incrementally inside the monolith)
exercises.expected_solution:
SAFEST to MOST DANGEROUS:
1. C: Parallel Run (part of Strangler Fig)
   Both systems active simultaneously
   Gradual traffic shift with instant rollback
   Dark launch validates correctness before user exposure

2. A: Strangler Fig
   Proxy layer + incremental migration
   Risk limited to % of traffic on new system
   Rollback: flip feature flag

3. E: In-Place Refactoring
   Safer than big bang: no separate system to maintain
   Risk: refactoring the live system (must not break existing behavior)
   Tests essential; blue-green deploy for each refactoring step

4. D: Feature Branch (1 year)
   12 months of divergence from main branch
   Merge conflicts: catastrophic
   Testing against 12-month-old production: irrelevant
   Better: trunk-based development with feature flags

5. B: Big Bang Rewrite
   Most dangerous: all-or-nothing risk
   If bugs found: old system gone, can't roll back
   Infamous failures: Netscape Navigator 6, HealthCare.gov v1
   Zerodha during Diwali: 3-day outage = ₹200+ crore loss

test_cases.expected:
SAFEST to MOST DANGEROUS:
1. C: Parallel Run (part of Strangler Fig)
   Both systems active simultaneously
   Gradual traffic shift with instant rollback
   Dark launch validates correctness before user exposure

2. A: Strangler Fig
   Proxy layer + incremental migration
   Risk limited to % of traffic on new system
   Rollback: flip feature flag

3. E: In-Place Refactoring
   Safer than big bang: no separate system to maintain
   Risk: refactoring the live system (must not break existing behavior)
   Tests essential; blue-green deploy for each refactoring step

4. D: Feature Branch (1 year)
   12 months of divergence from main branch
   Merge conflicts: catastrophic
   Testing against 12-month-old production: irrelevant
   Better: trunk-based development with feature flags

5. B: Big Bang Rewrite
   Most dangerous: all-or-nothing risk
   If bugs found: old system gone, can't roll back
   Infamous failures: Netscape Navigator 6, HealthCare.gov v1
   Zerodha during Diwali: 3-day outage = ₹200+ crore loss

hints: Big bang: no rollback path once users switched
Feature branch 1 year: merge conflicts destroy it
Strangler fig: risk capped at rollout %

exercises.title: Complete the feature flag router
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks in the feature flag routing logic.

blanks.hint: beta merchants always use new service

hints: hashCode(): deterministic — same merchant ID always produces same hash
Math.abs: hashCode can be negative
Set.contains(): O(1) lookup for beta merchants

exercises.title: FeatureFlagService with percentage rollout
exercises.instructions:
Write FeatureFlagService:
1. rolloutPercent: configurable (0-100)
2. betaMerchants: Set of always-on merchant IDs
3. isEnabled(merchantId): hash-based deterministic routing
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): beta always on, rollout=0 all off, rollout=100 all on
hints: rolloutPercent=0: Math.abs(hash)%100 < 0 → always false (all off)
rolloutPercent=100: Math.abs(hash)%100 < 100 → always true (all on)
Beta list: checked before percentage — overrides the rollout

exercises.title: StranglerFigFilter — OncePerRequestFilter
exercises.instructions:
Write StranglerFigFilter:
1. /api/orders endpoint: route based on FeatureFlagService
2. New service: forward request via WebClient
3. Legacy: chain.doFilter (pass to existing handlers)
4. @SpringBootTest: filter instantiates, flag=false → legacy path
hints: chain.doFilter: passes to legacy Spring controllers (no changes needed)
return after routeToNewService: don't call chain after forwarding to new service
X-Merchant-Id header: identifies which merchant for routing decision

exercises.title: Dark launch — parallel execution
exercises.instructions:
Write DarkLaunchService:
1. Execute on BOTH old and new service
2. User always sees old service result
3. Compare outputs, log mismatches
4. Micrometer counter: dark_launch.mismatch
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): same output → no mismatch, diff output → mismatch recorded
hints: User always gets legacyResult: dark launch is invisible to users
Mismatch counter: key metric — high mismatch rate = new service has bugs
Side effects: new service in dark launch must use dry-run mode (no DB writes, no emails)

exercises.title: Migration phase controller
exercises.instructions:
Write MigrationPhaseController:
1. Track current migration phase: DARK_LAUNCH, BETA_1_PERCENT, ROLLOUT_10, ROLLOUT_50, FULLY_MIGRATED
2. getNextPhase(): returns next phase
3. rollbackPhase(): always returns previous phase
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): phase progression, rollback, FULLY_MIGRATED is terminal
hints: ordinal(): enum position index (0-based)
PHASES.length-1: prevent going beyond FULLY_MIGRATED
rolloutPercent stored in enum: single source of truth for % → feature flag

exercises.title: API contract compatibility test
exercises.instructions:
Verify new service has same API contract as legacy:
1. LegacyOrderContract: expected fields (orderId, status, amount, merchantId)
2. NewServiceContractTest: call new service API, verify all fields present
3. Compatibility matrix: both return same response schema
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): legacy contract fields, new service contract matches
hints: Contract test: verify API response schema — not implementation
orderId will differ between old and new (generated independently)
status must be same: behavior contract, not just field presence

exercises.title: Nginx-based proxy routing configuration
exercises.instructions:
Design Nginx + Spring configuration for Strangler Fig routing:
1. Nginx upstream config: old_service + new_service
2. Spring @RestController: /api/orders (new service endpoint)
3. Migration-aware request handler: include X-Migrated-By header in response
4. @SpringBootTest: new service endpoint returns migration metadata
hints: X-Migrated-By header: helps identify which requests went to new service in logs
X-Service-Version: helps correlate incidents with deployment versions
Nginx routing: simpler than Spring filter for infrastructure-level routing

exercises.title: Migration monitoring dashboard
exercises.instructions:
Write MigrationMonitor:
1. Counter: orders.legacy (legacy system requests)
2. Counter: orders.new (new system requests)
3. Counter: migration.mismatch (output divergence during dark launch)
4. Gauge: migration.rollout_percent (current %)
5. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): counters increment correctly, gauge reads percent
hints: Gauge: reports current value at scrape time (not cumulative)
AtomicInteger: thread-safe mutable integer for gauge backing value
orders.legacy vs orders.new ratio: key migration health metric

exercises.title: Strangler Fig failure scenarios
exercises.instructions:
For each failure during migration (rollout at 25%), what is the correct response?
1. New service has 5x higher p99 latency than legacy
2. New service returns wrong P&L calculation (discovered in dark launch)
3. New service crashes and returns 500 for 2% of requests
4. New service has 0 errors but order IDs don't match legacy format
5. Legacy system goes down (not the new service)
exercises.expected_solution:
1. New service 5x higher p99 latency:
   Response: ROLLBACK — reduce rolloutPercent to 0%
   Instantly shifts all traffic back to legacy (feature flag flip)
   Investigate: profile new service, fix N+1 queries, add indexes
   Resume: only after p99 matches legacy within 2x

2. Wrong P&L calculation (found in dark launch):
   Response: FIX BEFORE CUTOVER — stay at 0% user traffic
   This is why dark launch exists: catch bugs before users see them
   Fix: update calculation logic, re-run dark launch comparison
   Resume cutover: only when dark launch shows 0 mismatches

3. New service returns 500 for 2% of requests:
   Response: ROLLBACK to 0% immediately
   25% of users × 2% failure rate = 0.5% of all users seeing errors
   Acceptable? No — even 0.5% error rate on payment creation is unacceptable
   Investigate: check logs for the 500 pattern, fix, re-test

4. New service has 0 errors but different order ID format:
   Response: DEPENDS on API consumers
   If clients depend on format (regex validation): BREAKING CHANGE, rollback and fix
   If format is opaque (treat as string): acceptable, document in changelog
   Best practice: new system should use same ID format during migration period
   Change format AFTER migration completes (separate change)

5. Legacy system goes down (not the new service):
   Response: ADVANCE rollout to 100% immediately
   New service is working fine — route ALL traffic to it
   Emergency promotion: this is the Strangler Fig's emergency escape hatch
   Monitor closely for first hour at 100%

test_cases.expected:
1. New service 5x higher p99 latency:
   Response: ROLLBACK — reduce rolloutPercent to 0%
   Instantly shifts all traffic back to legacy (feature flag flip)
   Investigate: profile new service, fix N+1 queries, add indexes
   Resume: only after p99 matches legacy within 2x

2. Wrong P&L calculation (found in dark launch):
   Response: FIX BEFORE CUTOVER — stay at 0% user traffic
   This is why dark launch exists: catch bugs before users see them
   Fix: update calculation logic, re-run dark launch comparison
   Resume cutover: only when dark launch shows 0 mismatches

3. New service returns 500 for 2% of requests:
   Response: ROLLBACK to 0% immediately
   25% of users × 2% failure rate = 0.5% of all users seeing errors
   Acceptable? No — even 0.5% error rate on payment creation is unacceptable
   Investigate: check logs for the 500 pattern, fix, re-test

4. New service has 0 errors but different order ID format:
   Response: DEPENDS on API consumers
   If clients depend on format (regex validation): BREAKING CHANGE, rollback and fix
   If format is opaque (treat as string): acceptable, document in changelog
   Best practice: new system should use same ID format during migration period
   Change format AFTER migration completes (separate change)

5. Legacy system goes down (not the new service):
   Response: ADVANCE rollout to 100% immediately
   New service is working fine — route ALL traffic to it
   Emergency promotion: this is the Strangler Fig's emergency escape hatch
   Monitor closely for first hour at 100%

hints: Feature flag flip: instant rollback without deployment
Dark launch: catch bugs BEFORE users see them (scenario 2)
Legacy goes down: emergency promote new service to 100%

exercises.title: Complete Strangler Fig proxy with monitoring
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Strangler Fig:
1. FeatureFlagService: rollout % + beta merchants
2. StranglerFigRouter: routes based on feature flag
3. MigrationMonitor: counters for legacy/new/mismatch
4. DarkLaunchComparator: compare outputs
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): routing correct, monitor increments, mismatch detected
hints: Beta merchant: always routes to new service (regardless of rollout %)
darkLaunch returns legacy result: user unaffected
mismatch counter: key signal that new service has bugs

exercises.title: Database migration strategy for Strangler Fig
exercises.instructions:
Old service uses MySQL, new uses PostgreSQL. Both need same data during migration.
1. DataSyncService: write to BOTH DBs (dual-write during migration)
2. DataReadStrategy: reads from old or new based on migration phase
3. DataMigrationJob: copy existing data from old to new
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): dual-write, read from correct source
hints: Dual-write: write to both, old is source of truth (new write can fail)
Read from new only when confident (after data sync validation)
Eventual consistency: new DB may lag slightly during bulk migration

exercises.title: Canary deployment with health-gated rollout
exercises.instructions:
Build automated rollout controller:
1. Advance rollout only if error rate < 1% for 5 minutes
2. Auto-rollback if error rate spikes above 2%
3. HealthGatedRolloutService: check metrics before advancing
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): healthy → advances, unhealthy → rollback, rollback idempotent
hints: Health gate: check error rate BEFORE advancing rollout
Auto-rollback: monitor error rate continuously; rollback if spike
migration.auto_rollback counter: alert ops when this fires

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha migration orchestration
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha migration system:
1. FeatureFlagService (rollout % + beta)
2. DarkLaunchComparator (compare outputs, return legacy)
3. HealthGatedAdvancement (auto-advance if healthy)
4. MigrationStatusReport: current phase + error rate + rollout %
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): dark launch compares, health gate advances, report accurate
hints: MigrationStatus record: immutable snapshot of migration state at a point in time
test order matters: darkLaunch test sets mismatchCount=1 before report test
rolloutPct>=0&&<=100: always valid regardless of other tests running

exercises.title: Complete M24 T4 Strangler Fig synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M24 T4 synthesis:
1. FeatureFlagService: rollout + beta merchants
2. StranglerFigRouter: route to legacy or new
3. DarkLaunchService: compare outputs
4. MigrationMonitor: counters
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): legacy path, beta→new, dark launch mismatch, rollout 100%→all new
6. All 4 pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M24 T4 Strangler Fig synthesis
hints: rollout=0: Math.abs(hash)%100 < 0 → always false
rollout=100: Math.abs(hash)%100 < 100 → always true
Test isolation: each test sets rolloutPercent (shared state!)
